Q1.bottleneck: Scalar aggregation loop with row-by-row processing
Q1.root_cause: No vectorization: 6M row scan with per-row hash lookups and arithmetic. std::unordered_map has poor cache locality (chaining). Low-cardinality group keys (4 groups) use expensive string keys instead of dictionary codes.
Q1.benchmark_gap: 4.1x slower than DuckDB (19ms). DuckDB likely uses vectorized execution with SIMD, perfect hashing for low-cardinality groups, and dictionary-encoded flags.

Q3.bottleneck: Multiple hash table operations and string comparisons in customer filter
Q3.root_cause: Three separate hash table operations (customer filter, orders join, lineitem join) with std::unordered_map overhead. String comparison on c_mktsegment='BUILDING' triggers strcmp for every customer row (150K calls). Hash table for orders is built after filtering but still uses std::unordered_map with chaining.
Q3.benchmark_gap: 5.0x slower than DuckDB (26ms). DuckDB likely dictionary-encodes c_mktsegment (integer comparison), uses cache-friendly linear probing hash table, and prefetches during probe phase.

Q6.root_cause: Three cascaded filters (date range, discount range, quantity threshold) evaluated row-by-row. Each filter requires memory load and branch. No SIMD to evaluate multiple predicates in parallel. Date range filter has ~14% selectivity (1 year out of 7) but is not exploited for early termination.
Q6.benchmark_gap: 3.0x slower than DuckDB (6ms). This is the smallest gap, but DuckDB likely uses SIMD filters to process 8-16 rows at once, reducing both memory bandwidth and branch mispredictions.

analysis.overall: GenDB is 3-5x slower than DuckDB across all queries. The root causes are: (1) Lack of vectorization - all operators use scalar row-at-a-time processing instead of batched execution, (2) Generic standard library data structures (std::unordered_map, std::string) instead of database-optimized implementations, (3) No SIMD for filters and aggregations, (4) String operations without dictionary encoding. Query execution time (262ms) is dominated by Q1 (51%) and Q3 (39%), making these the highest-priority targets. Load time (18.14s) is very high and should be addressed in parallel.

recommendations.description: Replace scalar filter loop in q6.cpp with SIMD-vectorized predicate evaluation using AVX2 intrinsics. Process 8 int32_t shipdate values simultaneously with _mm256_cmpgt_epi32, then apply discount and quantity filters with SIMD. Use selection vectors to materialize only qualifying rows. See simd.md for implementation patterns. Target lines 34-51 in queries/q6.cpp.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 2.5-3.5x speedup for Q6 (27ms → 8-11ms). SIMD filtering can process 8 rows per instruction, and Q6's highly selective filters (1.9% pass rate) benefit from parallel evaluation. This should bring Q6 close to DuckDB's 6ms.

recommendations.technique: Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality group keys
recommendations.description: Dictionary-encode l_returnflag and l_linestatus during data load (storage/storage.cpp). Store as uint8_t codes (0-3 for 4 combinations) instead of char. In q1.cpp aggregation, use direct array indexing instead of std::unordered_map: replace agg_table[key] with agg_array[key_code] where key_code = returnflag_code * 2 + linestatus_code. This eliminates hash computation and lookup overhead. See string-optimization.md for dictionary encoding patterns.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 1.5-2x speedup for Q1 (78ms → 40-52ms). Direct array indexing is 10-20x faster than hash table lookup for low cardinality (4 groups). Saves ~6M hash computations and lookups. Combined with SIMD (recommendation #4), could achieve 3-4x total speedup.

recommendations.description: Dictionary-encode c_mktsegment during customer table load. Store as uint8_t codes (0-4 for 5 market segments) instead of std::string. In q3.cpp line 52, replace string comparison customer.c_mktsegment[i] == "BUILDING" with integer comparison customer.c_mktsegment_code[i] == BUILDING_CODE (where BUILDING_CODE is the dictionary code for 'BUILDING', likely 0 or obtained from dictionary). This eliminates 150K strcmp calls. See string-optimization.md section on dictionary-coded comparisons.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 1.3-1.5x speedup for Q3 (102ms → 68-78ms). String comparison on 150K rows is expensive (~10-20 cycles per strcmp vs 1 cycle for integer comparison). Savings: ~2-3ms. Combined with hash table optimization (recommendation #6), total speedup could be 2-2.5x.

recommendations.description: Restructure q1.cpp to process data in chunks of 2048 rows at a time. For each chunk: (1) SIMD filter l_shipdate (8 values at once), (2) extract returnflag/linestatus codes into separate arrays, (3) aggregate arithmetic (l_extendedprice * (1-discount) * (1+tax)) with SIMD multiply/FMA operations, (4) update aggregate array. This improves cache locality (chunk fits in L1) and enables auto-vectorization. See vectorized-execution.md for chunk-based patterns.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 1.5-2x speedup on top of dictionary encoding (combined 3-4x from baseline 78ms → 20-26ms). Vectorized arithmetic and blocked execution improve IPC (instructions per cycle) and cache hit rate. Should bring Q1 very close to DuckDB's 19ms.

recommendations.technique: Parallel CSV parsing with memory pre-allocation
recommendations.description: In storage/storage.cpp: (1) Add table.reserve(estimated_rows) before parsing loop to pre-allocate vectors (avoids 20+ reallocations during push_back), (2) Use mmap() or fast I/O to read file in bulk, (3) Partition file by newlines and parse chunks in parallel across 4-8 threads, (4) Use string_view for date parsing instead of creating temporary strings. Estimated rows: lineitem=6M, orders=1.5M, customer=150K.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 3-5x speedup for data loading (18.14s → 3.6-6s). Pre-allocation eliminates realloc overhead. Parallel parsing utilizes multi-core. Fast I/O reduces system call overhead. Load time is currently 69x longer than query time - this is a major bottleneck.

recommendations.technique: Linear probing hash table with pre-sizing
recommendations.description: Replace std::unordered_map in q3.cpp with custom linear probing hash table. Pre-size table to 1.33x expected entries (75% load factor) to avoid resizing. Use power-of-2 size for fast modulo via bitwise AND. Store (hash, orderkey, orderdate, shippriority) in flat array. Linear probing: slot = (hash + probe_distance) & size_mask. See hash-join-variants.md for linear probing implementation. Target the order_info map on line 58.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 1.4-1.7x speedup for Q3 (68-78ms after dictionary encoding → 40-56ms). Linear probing has 10-20% better cache locality than chaining (std::unordered_map). Pre-sizing avoids rehashing overhead. Combined with dictionary encoding, total Q3 speedup: 2.4-3.3x from baseline.

recommendations.description: Pre-size the agg_table in q3.cpp line 68. Estimate distinct orderkeys after filtering: ~100K-300K groups (10-20% of 1.5M orders after date/customer filters). Pre-allocate: agg_table.reserve(estimated_groups * 1.5). This avoids rehashing during aggregation. Use same linear probing approach as recommendation #6 for consistency.
recommendations.expected_improvement: 1.2-1.3x additional speedup for Q3 on top of prior optimizations (40-56ms → 31-47ms). Avoids 3-5 rehash operations during aggregation. Combined with all prior Q3 optimizations, total speedup: 2.9-4.3x from baseline (102ms → 24-35ms), very close to DuckDB's 26ms.

recommendations.technique: Date filter with early termination via sorted data
recommendations.description: FUTURE: If lineitem is sorted by l_shipdate (as suggested in storage_design.json), binary search to find first row where l_shipdate > cutoff_date, then break loop early. This optimization requires storage-level changes (sorting during load). For now, note as future opportunity.
recommendations.expected_improvement: Minimal for Q1 since 98.6% of rows pass filter (only 1.4% filtered). Binary search overhead may exceed savings. Skip for iteration 1.

storage_changes.change: Dictionary-encode l_returnflag and l_linestatus during load. Add uint8_t l_returnflag_code and l_linestatus_code vectors. Build dictionary: {R:0, A:1, N:2} for returnflag, {F:0, O:1} for linestatus.
storage_changes.rationale: Q1 groups on these two low-cardinality fields (3x2=6 possible combinations, only 4 appear in data). Dictionary encoding enables direct array indexing instead of hash table lookup, eliminating 6M hash operations. See recommendation #2.

storage_changes.change: Dictionary-encode c_mktsegment during load. Add uint8_t c_mktsegment_code vector. Build dictionary: {AUTOMOBILE:0, BUILDING:1, FURNITURE:2, HOUSEHOLD:3, MACHINERY:4}.
storage_changes.rationale: Q3 filters on c_mktsegment = 'BUILDING'. Dictionary encoding replaces 150K strcmp calls with integer comparisons (1 cycle vs 10-20 cycles). 10-20x faster filter evaluation. See recommendation #3.

storage_changes.change: Pre-allocate vectors with reserve(6000000) before parsing loop in load_lineitem().
storage_changes.rationale: Avoids 20+ reallocations during push_back (each realloc copies entire array). Pre-allocation is standard practice for known table sizes. Part of load time optimization (recommendation #5).

storage_changes.change: Pre-allocate vectors with reserve(1500000) in load_orders().
storage_changes.rationale: Same as lineitem - avoid reallocation overhead during data load.

storage_changes.change: Pre-allocate vectors with reserve(150000) in load_customer().
storage_changes.rationale: Same as lineitem - avoid reallocation overhead during data load.

summary: Iteration 1 baseline is 3-5x slower than DuckDB. Primary bottlenecks: (1) Lack of vectorization/SIMD, (2) Generic std::unordered_map instead of cache-friendly linear probing, (3) String operations without dictionary encoding. Top priorities: SIMD filtering for Q6 (low-hanging fruit, 2.5-3.5x speedup), dictionary encoding for Q1/Q3 (enables direct array indexing and faster comparisons), and data load optimization (18s load time dominates 262ms query time). With these optimizations, expected performance: Q1: 78→20-26ms (3-4x), Q3: 102→24-35ms (3-4.3x), Q6: 27→8-11ms (2.5-3.5x), load time: 18s→3.6-6s (3-5x). This should bring GenDB to within 1.1-1.8x of DuckDB across all queries. Implementation strategy: Start with low-risk high-impact optimizations (dictionary encoding, SIMD for Q6) before tackling medium-risk changes (custom hash tables, vectorized execution). All recommendations avoid repeating failed optimizations (no history to avoid yet).
